cards.front: What is array decay in C++?
cards.back: Array decay is the implicit conversion of an array to a pointer to its first element. This happens when passing arrays to functions or in most expressions.

cards.text: When a C-style array decays, it becomes a pointer to the {{c1::first element}} of the array, losing all {{c2::size information}}

cards.question: What does this print and why?
cards.answer: Prints the size of a pointer (typically 4 or 8 bytes), not 20 bytes
cards.explanation: The array decayed to a pointer when passed to the function. sizeof(arr) in the function measures the pointer, not the array.

cards.text: To prevent array decay when passing to a function, pass by {{c1::reference}}: void func({{c2::int (&arr)[5]}})

cards.front: What information is lost when a C-style array decays to a pointer?
cards.back: The array's length information is lost. The pointer only knows the address of the first element, not how many elements exist in the array.

cards.text: The function parameters int arr[] and {{c1::int* arr}} are {{c2::exactly equivalent}} - both receive a pointer

cards.question: What's misleading about this function signature?
cards.answer: The [5] is ignored - it's treated as int* arr. You can pass arrays of any size.
cards.explanation: The array size in function parameters is cosmetic and not enforced. It still decays to a pointer.

Solutions to array decay problems:
  • Use std::array instead of C-style arrays
  • Pass by reference: void func(int (&arr)[5])
  • Use templates for size preservation
  • Pass size as separate parameter alongside pointer
Best: avoid C-style arrays entirely.

cards.text: To write a function that accepts C-style arrays of any size without decay, use a {{c1::template}} with a {{c2::non-type parameter}} for the size

cards.front: Does std::array decay to a pointer when passed to functions?
cards.back: No, std::array does not decay. When passed by value, it's copied. When passed by reference, it maintains its type and size information.

cards.text: To get a pointer to the first element explicitly without relying on decay, use {{c1::&arr[0]}} or the array name itself

cards.front: In what contexts does array decay NOT occur?
cards.back:
Array decay doesn't occur when:
  • Using sizeof on the array
  • Taking address with & operator
  • Initializing a reference to the array
  • In range-based for loops (C++11+)

cards.question: Why pass both pointer and size?
cards.answer: Because array decays to pointer, losing size info. Must pass size separately.
cards.explanation: This is the common C-style pattern to work around decay. Modern C++ should use std::array or std::span (C++20) instead.
